title: Terrie Moffitt: Young Children's Self-Control and the Health ...
text:
Today
what I want to do is
tell you about one
project that my research team has
been working on for about the past four
years
on self control, health, wealth, and public
safety.
Overall our research aims to find
ways to prepare today's children for
tomorrow
and there are several reasons why we
think this preparation of today's
children
is important. First, we learn from
demographers
that because of the falling birthrate worldwide
children are going to become
increasingly rare
an increasingly valuable. At the same
time
there are more and more of us old people.
I just had my sixtieth birthday on
Monday so I'm sixty years and four days
and now I'm counting myself among the old
people.
I'm trying to get used to the idea.
That means that each young
worker will support more and more
old people and that's called the support
ratio.
I'll tell you a bit more about it in a
moment. 
Then the last fact we've learned from
demographers is about life expectancy.
Its growing longer and longer
and this really means that today's
children will have to prepare
to support us in our old age
but also to prepare for a longer old age
for themselves. I'll tell you about the
support ratio.
What you see here is the classic age
pyramid. The blue part shows for males
and the pink part for females.
The link for the rows represents the
size the population
in each age group. At the bottom of the
slide you see how many children there
are and
at the top how many old people and in
the middle
it shows you how many people are adult
workers.
These are data from Brazil which I'm
showing you as an example because Brazil
make such a beautiful age
pyramid at the moment. You see that in
the year 2000
most of the Brazilian population were
young people
and 2020 we see those children moving up
the pyramid
and becoming adult workers and by 2050
most of Brazil's population will be old
people.
Now I showed you the same population pyramid for Western Europe
including the Netherlands and what you
see immediately
is that this is already happened to us.
Were well ahead
of Brazil. Every child is already
more and more valuable today. So here
I'll show you and outline of the
lecture so you can follow along to see
how the lecture is progressing.
and this is so that if you become
terribly bored
you can see it will not take very much
longer, so you can count the time coming 
until the cocktail reception.
So what I want to do first is show you some
evidence from the Dunedin study that
childhood self control predicts success and
failure in adult life
above and beyond intelligence and family
wealth.
Now this is important because everyone
already knows that success in life
follows from high intelligence and from
good family socioeconomic resources.
You heard yesterday afternoon in one
of the major 
sessions about the importance of socioeconomic status
for success in life but it's also
known that it's very difficult
if not impossible to eliminate the wide
differences between people
on their IQ's and their social class of
origin:
In contrast, self control skills
are thought to be something that might
be teachable. Now at this point I have to
also pause and tell you that one reason we
did this research
is because of colleagues who
were professors of economics
challenged me to study self control. They
said, 'You've got
data on self control in the Dunedin study.
Why don't you write them up. Why don't you
see if childhood self control predicts
adult outcomes?'
And I said, 'I've spent my whole career
studying intelligence and
neuropsychological functions and
executive function.
I know that intelligence is where it's
at and if you control for intelligence
 
self control will become unimportant.' So
they didn't believe me, these pesky
economist
and we made a 100 dollar wager. I
wagered that IQ would be the most
important variable in the dataset; 
they wagered on self control and thus I did 
the work I'll tell you about tonight. So
today there's a lot of new scientific
interest in self control by researchers
and policymakers.
That's because it's thought to be
more necessary
now than ever before in world history.
Historians are telling us that
we need our self control to avoid
becoming obese because we live
for the first time in human history in era
of easy food availability. We need it to
maintain our fitness
because for the first time in human
history our jobs are sedentary.
We need our willpower to sustain our
marriages because it's so easy to get a
divorce today.
We need our self-discipline to prevent
addiction
because illicit an addictive substances
are
easily accessible all around us and not
merely in
Amsterdam. We need it to resist
spending. If you go down to the big
shopping district you'll see what I mean
but we live in an era of sophisticated
marketing
more so than ever before. And now 
on top of all this depressing stuff we
have to save for
our own old age because
now we live in a era without guaranteed pensions.
So self control is currently under intense
study by many of the behavioral sciences.
If you visited the posters out here
today you would see many titles having
to do with self control.
Many of the behavioral sciences are
using
different concepts and different
measurement approaches. For example
personality psychologists
study self control, social psychologists study self control, child psychologist
even economists. Neurosciences study
executive functions. Psychiatrytends
to focus in on
attention hyperactivity disorder and
even Management Science in Business
Schools
studying self-discipline in the service
of leadership.
So it's a very widely studied 
construct in the the behavioral sciences
today.
What I would like to do now
is introduce you to the Dunedin
study, which is
where we've done our work. So we need to
leave the Netherlands and travel to New
Zealand.
Note that New Zealand as opposed to Old Zealand,
which is where we sit today. 
New Zealand was first described by a
Dutch explorer. 
That's why we have the two
names
opposite to each other. So this is 
the design of the Dunedin study. We began
by studying all the babies born in one
city in one
year - 1972-1973
and 1,037 of them who
were eligible and agreed to take part. 
The cohort represents the full
variation in the population -- all
walks of life
and all ability levels. If you look on
the left hand side of the slide what you
see is that
each age that the study members have
come into our research unit, 
into the clinic for full day
assessment
and if you look down to the bottom
right hand part  of the slide you
see an important detail -- that the last
time we saw them when they were 38 years
old in 2012, ninety-five percent of the
original birth cohort still took part.
This means that those who have been
unsuccessful in life have not dropped
out of the study
along the way so the findings that
I'll show you still represent
the whole population of New Zealand's South Island.
We measured each child's self control
during the first
decade of life by assessing the sorts
of qualities that you see here:
impulsive, 
acts without thinking,  can't wait his or
her turn,
has a low frustration tolerance, dislikes
effortful tasks,
fleeting attention, lacks
persistence,
goes for the risky thing, requires
constant attention
and motivation from an adult. So that's
an example of the kinds
of items and ratings that were collected
by the team in New Zealand
during the first decade of life. 
Of course every child shows
poor self control at some point.
We all know about the terrible twos. Small children haven't got that much
self control and that's developmentally
typical.
So for the research that we're doing now
we wanted to define
self control in terms of its 
persistence and pervasiveness. So we 
made a composite or ratings 
that were collected by the Dunedin team.
We asked if the child's style of self
control
had persisted across ages 3-5 7
9 and 11 years
and we asked that it was agreed upon by
multiple reporters
so we had staff observations of the
child self control
during the day spent at the clinic. We
had parents reports on repeated parent
checklist in each of those ages
four different teachers reports at age
5, 7, 9 and 11, and then when the children were 11 years we also interviewed them
about their own self control, once they
were old enough to reflect on that. 
When I speak of course of control 
I don't mean just a single
instance or a temper tantrum.
I mean a persistent and cross situational
pattern of self control. Now what are the
consequences
of childhood self control. We want to
move forward now for about 30
years. In the Dunedin study we invest a lot of
our assessment time measuring health.
When our study participants visit the
research unit we assess their health
using a full day of different medical
tests and examinations.
For example, we assess their
cardiovascular fitness. 
We assess their cardiovascular health.
This is an endothelial function test. 
Anthropometrics and body fat. Blood pressure. 
There's a respiratory team that assesses their lung and
pulmonary function.
And this is every study member's
favorite, the last thing at the end of
the day
is they get to see the dentist for a complete dental exam.
And then we draw blood from
them at the same time
every day and that we use for laboratory
test such as sexually transmitted
infections or
systemic inflammation. 
For this talk I want to make
one summary measure to summarize each
person's health.
So accounted whether the study
members had clinically abnormal
levels on metabolic abnormalities, and
that includes
obesity; on periodontal disease
as measured by the dentist; on sexually
transmitted infections;
on systemic inflammation
in blood as measured by c-reactive protein
and respiratory 
difficulties as measured by the
respiratory team. 
We just counted up the number of health problems,
on 
health assessments, on which a study
member fell into the clinically
abnormal range already by the age of 38
years.
This slide shows you
the first finding. Poor childhood self
control was linked to the number of
these health problems that each study
member had
in their late 30s and I'll just walk you
through the slide because you'll see
several of these and I want to make sure
that you understand them. Across the
bottom of the slide you see the cohort of
1,000 children divided into five
equally sized groups, from low self
control to high self control. 
Each of the dark gray bars represents a
group containing 200 children.
The height the chart indicates the
number of health problems that I just
showed you,
just a simple count have health problems
in the clinical range.
What I'd like you to notice is the
the shape of the gradient.
The poorer the child's self control in the
first decade of life,
the poorer their health at age 38.
So I'd like you to keep your our eye on
this gradient
because we're gonna see it over and over
and until you get quite bored with it
but I think it's important and I'll
explain why at the end of the talk.
One way to think about how self
control is affecting these study members'
health
as they grow up is to think about the pace of
aging. Already in the thirties there's large
individual differences
in how rapidly people are aging. These
young women are all the same
chronological age. Their photographs are
taken within 60 days of their 38th
birthday.
So whether we consider the health of their bodies,
the health of their brains, the health of 
their faces, some people are
aging faster than others and at this
point
I wanted to show you a new project that
we've been working on just for the last
six months
and this is to use our health
biomarkers from the study
to track the process of age-related
change
in each Dunedin study member's life
over their twenties and thirties and
we'll continue this
into their 40s and 50s in future. Now
the theory that we're using is from
gerontology
and that is the concept that aging is a
gradual
and correlated simultaneous
deterioration
across all systems of the body. It's kind of
discouraging, but there's the theory. 
The most discouraging thing is that this deterioration, this 
gradual, 
and correlated deterioration
happens years before you see any
age-related disease onset so you should
be able to detect it
in people in their 20s, 30s and 40s.
I'll show you how we're doing that.
Here you see the 18 biomarkers that
we've measured
repeatedly since 1998 in the course of
the study
and you can see that they are following
the gerontology theory
crossing many bodily systems. So we have gum disease, we have telomere
length,
c-reactive protein, cardiorespiratory
fitness
creatinine, BMI, waist hip ratio,
blood pressure, the FEV
measures have long pulmonary function,
cholesterol, triglycerides and so forth
for the metabolic syndrome  and HB
a1c
for diabetes risk so 18 different
biomarkers that we've been tracking.
And this shows you what's happening for
the data in the cohort
ages 26, 32, and 38. What you see is a
general trend
in which the mean levels of each of these
biomarkers has been
getting higher over that dozens years that we've been tracking them
and this shows that the Dundedin study
members' bodies are aging.
Now any given individual can show
a peak in any given biomarker at any
given
age perhaps just because they have
the flu or some other short term
temporary illness but what you see here
is a more general trend for,
as the theory of gerontology predicts, that
all of the bodily systems will be
deteriorating together
in a correlated way.
So we've developed an algorithm that
combines the biomarkers to
estimate each individual's biological
pace of aging, and here I show you the
result of that algorithm so far
for the five groups of Dunedin study members defined based on their
their level of self control
as a child in the first decade of life.
The dashed line across the middle of the
slide equals age 38.
So since each person was assessed
within 60 days
of their 38th birthday you can see how
the biomarker profiles
for the sample as a norm is at age
38.
If you look on the left-hand part of the slide you see the lowest self control
group
is biologically older than 38
already. They're almost 39 years old on
these biomarkers.
If you look on the right the highest
self control children are staying young
their mean biological age is about 37.
So the two groups are about one
and a half years apart
and this difference between them is
expected to widen
as they continue to age. Now I want to
tell you a bit about drug addiction
We interview each of the study members
about substance dependence
according to DSM 5 of DSM 4 criteria
with a clinical psychologist. 
For this talk this evening what I did was
simply count up the number of different
substances that each of our study
members
is dependent upon. They could be
dependent upon tobacco,
alcohol, cannabis or other street and
prescription drugs
and I'm gonna show you a simple count. Here you see the finding. 
The red line shows you that the
children with poorer self control
said as adults that they were addicted
to more different substances
than the children who had better self
control. If you look at the blue line,
these are reports provided about the
study members by people who knew them
well.
Each study member nominates three people who know them well and we send
out questionnaires to those people.They
tend to be partners,
friends,
roommates, some parents, some coworkers, some siblings.
We consult these informants
because we wanted an independent way to
verify findings that are based on
self-reports
especially in the event that you might
not trust
self-reports as might be wise in the
matter of addiction to illegal drugs.
But what you see is the blue line and
the red line
look very similar. So whether we
interview the study members about their
addiction problems
or whether we asked their informants if
they have problems with drugs and
alcohol
we see the same thing. As adults children
with poor self control
who are on the left of the slide have
more addiction problems.
Now we'll move onto wealth measures.
First I'll show you standard indicators of
income and the prestige of the study
members
occupations. The children with
poor self control
in their late thirties were earning less
money then they're more
self-controlled peers. That's the light blue line. And they also had occupations that
were less prestigious
and less skilled. That's the dark blue line. 
Next, 
I'll talk about financial planfulness for
the future. Since the sample is aging and
since we all must now save for our
old age, this becomes more important. We
interviewed the study members for about
a half an hour
about their attitudes towards saving and
their savings behavior
asking them questions about how
important is it to them to save for the
future,
do they save money by putting it away and
each month not touching it,
do they find themselves living from
paycheck to paycheck.
And then we also interviewed them about
financial building blocks.
Did they already own their own home or
apartment? Did they have any investments, 
or do they have a retirement plan?
Things of that nature. That suggests that
they are already
in their 30s thinking ahead to prepare
for their financial future.
And the slide shows you that children
with less self control
when they reach their late thirties were
less oriented toward saving
and had accrued fewer assets as building
blocks for their financial future.
 
I'm gonna tell you now about credit ratings
The range on credit ratings
is from 100 to 1000.
I don't know if you have credit rating
system in the country where you live
but we do have one in the United States
and they have one in New Zealand.
What credit ratings do is they
keep a track
of any kind of bills that you don't pay
or any financial
indiscretions that you have.
If you have anything that you buy
and then it's
repossessed, if you've become bankrupt
and you receive a rating
that is held about you. So we were
able to get the Dunedin study member's
permission to download their credit
ratings from the credit rating company
that monitors New Zealand and Australia
and
what we found is that on this range
from 100 to 1000, 
700, they tell us,  
is a good credit rating. Below a credit
rating of 700, 
a person will have difficulty getting
credit without clear 
collateral. This means that you'll have
difficulty borrowing money to buy a
house or an apartment
or to start a business. But also it means
more than that.
Your access to life insurance is priced
based on your credit rating.
In the United States the price of your
health insurance and your access to
health insurance
is priced based on your credit rating, 
also
automobile insurance. Even when getting a mobile phone contract,
they check your credit
rating first.
So we did some research on this in a
paper that was published
in PNAS last autumn
and what you see here is the study
members who have the lowest self control
also had the lowest credit ratings
and this suggests that they're going to have
great difficulty
with raising capital as they move
forward at this stage of life
where they want to be buying houses and 
starting businesses. Now we want to look
at their
criminal behavior. We
We get information about study members court convictions at all
the courts in New Zealand and Australia
by searching the central police computer
of the New Zealand police. This shows
you the percentage of the cohort members
in our sample who have been convicted of
a crime in criminal court by their late
thirties.
The convictions don't include traffic offenses.
These are criminal offenses. As adults children with poor self
control
were more likely to have been convicted
of a crime. Now we'll move on to
parenting.
We ask if poor childhood self
control
also has intergenerational effects so
by their late thirties about seventy-five percent of the Dunedin
study members had already become
a parent and when any study member has a
first child who reaches the age of three
we carry out a home visit
and what is collected at the home
visit is video tapes of parent-child
interaction
according to a special paradigm. These
videotapes are then sent to a team of
experts on parenting who code the video
tapes for the study members'
warmth toward their toddler, their
sensitivity to the child
and the stimulation that they give their
child for child development.
This work is directed by Jay Belsky who is
here at the conference this week so if
you're interested in
in this part of the data please talk to
Jay. The coders that he uses
are kept blind to all other information
about the study members.
This slide shows you that the left
self control
a study member had in childhood that
this translated directly to the less 
skilled parenting
when the study members grew up and had
their own child
at the age of three so less self controlled
children
turn out as adults to be less warm, less sensitive to their child's needs
and provide a less stimulating environment
for their child to develop
Once again this follows that
familiar gradient.
By now you're probably saying to yourself, 'Just a just a minute here. I bet the
reason that poor self control seems to
make such a big difference
in these young people's lives is because
children with poor self control tend to
come from poor homes
or to have low intelligence or maybe
they're all boys
and they probably have ADHD.' That's what I would have thought, too.
Those are great alternative explanations
but we checked him
and they don't explain the facts. The
gradients that I have shown you on these
slides today
were statistically adjusted for social
class
and for the children's intelligence
scores. This is the point where I lost
the hundred dollars
wager with the economists. The
self control gradient is the same among
children from high-income families,
children with above-average IQ's, it's
the same
within girls and within boys. All the
girls have more self control
than all the boys are over here on self
control. But within
sex the associations between
self control in childhood an adult
outcomes remain
similar. We also have repeated all
the analyses I've shown you today
and took out the 61 children from the
cohort 
who had been diagnosed with ADHD as a
child
and the findings were still the
same.
Now I want to tell you a little bit
about these young people's
period of adolescence. We all chuckled when we saw the slide when they were 13
years old so that's where we're going to go at the moment so. So far I've
emphasized childhood.
We also look at the Dunedin
study members' teen years
and this is because it's possible that
all these poor
adult outcomes happened to low self
control children who made mistakes
as a teenager and if that's true
it would mean that the poor outcomes
could be reversed
with prevention programs implemented in
secondary schools
and that's important because they're
already so many prevention programs
going on in secondary schools. What
you see here
is, yes,  the Dunedin children with the
lowest self control
did make the most mistakes as teenagers.
They were the most likely to start
smoking cigarettes,
they were the most likely to drop out of
high school, and they were the most
likely to have an
unplanned baby. What I'd like you to now is magine a utopian world where no
teenagers ever smoke, none of them drop
out of school,
none of them have an unplanned pregnancy. So to see if these kinds of adolescent
mistakes accounted for all of the poor
adult outcomes
we look at the Dunedin study members who
have this kind of
utopian adolescence -  the subgroup who did
not smoke
did not drop out of school and did not
have a baby. This is what we found
for their health
outcomes. The green line on the slide
shows you the self control
gradient of health problems that you
already saw
a few slides back. So thiis is the
simple relationship between self control
and number of health problems at age 38
in the fall cohort. The yellow line shows
you the same effects
on health in the utopian subsample.
It's interesting because
all of these utopian teenagers had fewer
health problems as adults.
What that means is the whole gradient
was moved downward for them
and I think that's far important because
it hints
what can be achieved with successful
interventions in secondary schools
but what you also see is that the
yellow gradient retains the same shape
as the green gradient
and this means that even among those who
did not make any mistake as a teenager,
or at least not the mistakes we counted, 
childhood self control still predicts
how many health problems they will
develop years later as an adult.
This is the same kind of 
analysis, now substituting
wealth as the outcome and you see the same result.
All of these utopian teenagers have
higher incomes
as adults so the entire gradient for
income was moved
upwards for them. But it's still the
same gradient shape
and that means that even among those who
did not make mistakes as teenagers
childhood self control was still
important for how much money
they would be able to earn years later
as adults.
At this point we want to do a little
work to isolate whether self control
is the active ingredient. I'm still
clinging onto IQ and wanting to win back
by one hundred dollars.
so I'm trying to push this in another
sample.
In the Dunedin study we used statistical controls for family income in
order to show that a child's self control
was important beyond his or her family
income
but as you know there's a lot more to
family life than just
money so we wanted another way to isolate
self control
as the active ingredient. So let's look
within one family.
What you can do is compare two siblings.
Sibling 1
there at the top, the one with the poorer
self control shown running around
who is theoretically at risk for
poorer later outcomes as compared to the
other sibling who has relatively better
self control and is very sweetly
reading a book
shown on the bottom line and the question
is would these two children have
different outcomes
even though they grow up in the same
home with the same parents.
This question gives me an opportunity
to tell you about our
other newer longitudinal study so we
must leave New Zealand now
and travel back to the Northern
Hemisphere to Britain.
In the 1990s
we move to England and we began a
new longitudinal study using the
same
general model as the Dunedin study but
this time
following a sample of twins. We enrolled a national sample of twins
born
in the UK. Here you see
them on their first day of school
in 1999 and there
are workers from that study who are here.
Tim Mathews is a PhD student in
particular so Tim, if you're there you
might raise your hand. He's been
working very hard on this study this
year.
It's called the Environmental Risk
Longitudinal Study.
Its a 1994-95 
birth cohort, 2,232 twins growing up in
1100 and some families, 
nationally representative UK.
We oversampled twins born to teenage
mothers to get twins growing up in
social disadvantage
and we undersampled twins born to older
mothers who used
 
assisted reproduction in order to
avoid having a twin sample
that was raised by older, more
educated mothers.
Half the twins are dizygotic. Half are monozygotic.
We followed them up at birth, age 5, age 7, age 10,
age 12 and just finished seeing them in
December at age
eighteen years and 93 percent of the
twins took part
as 18-year-olds. So kudos to the London
team for pulling that off.
Now we want to
measure their self control at age 5.
We used the same kind of measurement
approaches I had shown you
in the Dunedin study. We have parent
measures, we have the research worker
who does the home visit measures and the teacher
does measures of this kind of 
evidence of variation in self control.
I should also pause to tell you that
the monozygotic twins are more alike on
self control than the dizygotic twins
so there is evidence from the study of genetic
influence. Now we fast forward to their
adolescence. All we did was we compared
just using the dizygotic twins so
that we could get twins who are very
different from each other on self
control.
We compared these dizygotic twins within each pair
on problems when they reached
adolescence. We chose to count school
failure
because it's our best predictor about
adult wealth. We chose to
count smoking because it's our best
predictor in adolescence of
adult health and we chose to count
juvenile delinquency
because it's our best adolescent
predictor of adult criminal convictions
and on average in this simple depiction
we see the twin with the lower self
control at age five
had more of these kinds of problems in
secondary school.
That's shown with the left bar. And this is
despite having grown up
in the same home with the same parents
the same neighborhood,
the same school and in most cases in the
same classroom.
We also added statistical
controls here
for any differences between the two
twins in birthweight
or in their IQ scores. 
From this analysis we learn it's not
merely the family into which you're born
that matters,
it's whether you're able to develop self
control
skills that counts as well.
Next we wanted to look at the cost to
society.
I've shown you that self control affects
the lives of individuals.
We also wanted to evaluate the
importance of childhood self control for
an individual's nation. What's the cost
of poor self control
to everybody. We looked at the cost of
self control
in schools and classrooms. When E-risk  twins were twelve-year-olds
we asked their teachers, 'How much effort
does it take you to have
this child in your classroom?' We
said
on the questionnaires, 'Compared to their
classmates how often must you keep this
child's attention on
task? How often must you act to curb
disruptive behavior by this child?
How often does this child's behavior
make it frustrating
to work with him or her in the classroom?How often does this child need one to one
interaction from you?' How often must you
give this child
extra encouragement to get him or her to
take part.' Interestingly, when we were
looking for a measure of
teacher effort, we couldn't find one in
the educational literature or the
developmental psychology literature
so this is not an established,
standardized, fully normed measure of
teacher
effort. My mother is a teacher and I just
asked her to make a list for me of
the things that got up her nose in the
classroom. 
So this is Mrs. Moffett's pretty good
measure of teacher
effort.  So here you see our old familiar
gradient. The less self control the child
had at age 5
the more effort the teacher said she had
to use to control that child seven years
later
when they entered secondary schoo. The
data points are adjusted for the child's
IQ
and the family's social class. The pupils with low self control
take teachers effort and energy away from
teaching
other pupils and the classroom. This
suggests that
low self control children may have a
knock-on effect of reducing teacher's
job satisfaction,
increasing teacher turnover, losing
teachers
from the work force
 and this means it has
potentially important costs to our
education system.
Now I want to show you
economic measures of the cost of low
self control to government and to answer
this question now we return to New
Zealand.
We collaborated with the New Zealand
government
in order to look at records of social
welfare benefits that have been used by
our study members
over the years. We learned three things
from doing this.
First almost half of our study members
in Dunedin have received some social
welfare
benefits between the age of 21 and 38.
That's a lot of people
but New Zealand has a generous social
welfare system and people use it.
Next, what we see is 
although childhood self control did not
predict whether someone needed a
government benefit,
what it did predict is how long
adults stay on government benefits. So
we have their social welfare benefits
month by month and we see that on
average Dunedin children with the
poorest self control
were likely to be supported by
government social welfare benefits
for around four to six years. Those with
the best
self can best self control, if they
needed helping hand 
from government,
were able to get off of the benefits
within a year.
So clearly the cost of poor self control
to the New Zealand taxpayer is not
trivial
and the cost is even greater to
political
attitudes. We know from surveys that
most taxpayers want their country to
have a safety net
but what they do not want is for social
welfare recipients to become dependent
upon welfare benefits
and stay on them for many years. This
kind of thing
undermines public support for the social
welfare system.
But are they happy? I'm often

all have

That would suggest a bad thing. If it

who lack creativity and spontaneity
and
are incapable of personal
happiness.
This frightened me a little bit

This slide shows you that most people in

all are those on the right, those who

life as a child with high self control.
Now
we looked at creativity also and I don't

an extensive

control
is not related to whether a study member
plays music

or books or plays or does visual art

It's not related to whether they enter

or become a scientist or play

Self control did not explain who engaged
in achievements but
what self control predicted is whether

whether their

whether their artworks have been sold

So it's not creativity per se that's

are self control but it seems to be
the self-discipline
that converts creativity into 
a product. So that's what we're
finding
in relation to creativity. 
What are the implications of all this?
You've seen already that self control in
early childhood predicts many important
outcomes.
If it were possible to improve self
control skills
doing this might lower costs to
government and taxpayers of crime control,
health care delivery, and social welfare
benefits
and even education children who begin
their life by learning better self
control should in their lives healthier
and better prepared financially
for retirement an dold age and this would
reduce
dependency in old age and improve
quality of longer lives.
What about for the next generation of
children?
We saw that the study members with low
self control were less warm,
less sensitive to their own
three-year-old child. We also saw that
they were likely to have low incomes, to
be struggling financially,
to have poor physical health, to have a
criminal record
and to be substance abusers. This is not
the ideal parenting situation for
rearing the next generation
of children. So
when to intervene? Childhood or
adolescence? There's a great deal of
debate about this in the literature,
especially in the economic literature. 
When you get the best return on
investment? We showed you that
preventing adolescents from making
mistakes might be one window of
opportunity.
If we could prevent them from starting
smoking, dropping out of school
and getting pregnant that would go a
long way.
But we also saw that it would get us only
about
half the way there and that suggests that earlier intervention
is going to be important too.
So if you remember that gradient, I said
I would mention why I think it's
important.
When we published the gradient first in
2011 many people found it surprising.
It's gotten a lot of attention from
academics and from researchers and
policymakers.
Most people, including me when I first
made this wager,
would have thought that there are a few
children who lack self control
and that maybe these children are just
the small number who have
diagnosable attention deficit
hyperactivity disorder
or disorder of impulse control such as
conduct disorder
and that these few children need
targeted
individual treatment by a trained mental
health professional.
But our findings suggest something
different and they're persuading me,
although I'm a trained mental health
professional, that I might be
out of a job. So I think that
you've got to ask yourself, 'Would targeted
intervention for a few
do the trick or do we need to consider
universal interventions for all?'
I showed you that even children who are
above average I'm self control could
benefit from better self control skills.
The gradient was there for them.
Even intelligent children from
well-to-do homes
could benefit from better self control
skills. The gradient looked the same for them.
The gradient usually is taken to imply
the advantages of universal
enhancement and universal programs are
also politically popular
because taxpayers don't mind paying for
them if
everyone's child is going to benefit. So
what should we say about
a universal intervention. There
are lots of different ways to think
about this
and I just want to give you one example
and this is from Sesame Street
children's television and it's a program
called 'For Me,
For You, For Later' and in this
program
the puppets Elmo and Grover and Cookie
Monster show children how to save money
and how to delay gratification. They show
them how to think ahead to the future to
get something they
really really want by waiting.
The puppets identify a toy that they
really really want
and they understand that they it need
money to buy it
and so they start taking on little
tasks and chores such as raking leaves or
watering plants for the adults on the
Sesame Street show.
The adults give each of them three
pennies each time they do it
a chore and they put 
one penny in the jar for me, one
in the jar for you - that's for charity
and one in the jar for later - that's for teaching self control. 
The website is shown there. They
disseminate materials for parents and
teachers to reinforce the contents of
the television program at home
and the Sesame Street
designers tell me
that the program is based on the
principle that money
is the strongest motivator for learning
and saving money
is the best way to teach self control
even for preschool
children. So
how should we think about universal
intervention? To understand it I like
to think about our recent history.
Modern countries like the Netherlands
entered the 20th century with a huge
education gap. Only an elite few went to university
and most people only had eight years of
schooling.
Many citizens didn't know how to read at
all.
Teaching literacy to the whole
population
became a societal goal for the early
years of the nineteen hundreds
in the countries that are developed
today, and it was a huge success.
It changed the quality of life and
increased prosperity
for entire nations. Today we have another,
newer factor in early life that seems to
influence
life success for children. Most everybody
learns to read now.
That program has succeeded, but
as I showed you at the beginning of the
talk, self control has come to mean more
today
than it did in the past and a person's
level of health and wealth
now follow in part from the self control
skills
they were able to master as children. The
gradient that I showed you means that
teaching better self control skills to
the whole population of children
and expecting better self control as a
cultural
adaptation might become an interesting
societal goal
for this century. All this brings us an
opportunity to initiate a civic dialogue
about the kind of society we want in the
future
and for tomorrow's children. I hope this
lecture
will stimulate the audience to think
about what future you want for children
growing up
in your home country.
